lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Thus with imagined wing our swift scene flies

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: In motion of no less celerity

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Than that of thought. Suppose that you have seen

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The well-appointed king at Hampton pier

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Embark his royalty; and his brave fleet

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With silken streamers the young Phoebus fanning:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Play with your fancies, and in them behold

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Upon the hempen tackle ship-boys climbing;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Hear the shrill whistle which doth order give

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To sounds confused; behold the threaden sails,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Borne with the invisible and creeping wind,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Draw the huge bottoms through the furrow’d sea,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Breasting the lofty surge: O, do but think

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: You stand upon the rivage and behold

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: A city on the inconstant billows dancing;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: For so appears this fleet majestical,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Holding due course to Harfleur. Follow, follow:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Grapple your minds to sternage of this navy,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And leave your England, as dead midnight still,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Guarded with grandsires, babies and old women,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Either past or not arrived to pith and puissance;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: For who is he, whose chin is but enrich’d

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With one appearing hair, that will not follow

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: These cull’d and choice-drawn cavaliers to France?

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Work, work your thoughts, and therein see a siege;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Behold the ordnance on their carriages,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With fatal mouths gaping on girded Harfleur.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Suppose the ambassador from the French comes back;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Tells Harry that the king doth offer him

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Katharine his daughter, and with her, to dowry,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Some petty and unprofitable dukedoms.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The offer likes not: and the nimble gunner

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With linstock now the devilish cannon touches,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And down goes all before them. Still be kind,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And eke out our performance with your mind.

glosses.definition: the audience's imagination as wings — the Chorus continues its central metaphor
swiftness; speed
properly equipped and fitted out for war
Southampton's dock — the embarkation port
silk pennants fluttering from the masts
the streamers fanning the rising sun — Phoebus Apollo is the sun-god
the hemp ropes of the ship's rigging
the boatswain's pipe — signalling orders
canvas sails — 'threaden' = made of thread/cloth
large ship hulls
cutting through tall waves chest-first
shore; coastline (French loan-word)
restless waves
the French port town Henry besieges — modern Le Havre is built beside it

glosses.phrase: Grapple your minds to sternage of this navy
glosses.definition: hook your imagination to the stern of this fleet — 'sternage' = the fleet's stern, the following ships
England's wartime skeleton population — the able-bodied are abroad
vigor and military power
picked, handpicked horsemen — an elite selection
cannons mounted on wheeled carriages

glosses.phrase: fatal mouths gaping on girded Harfleur
glosses.definition: deadly cannon mouths aimed at besieged Harfleur — 'girded' = surrounded by a ring of troops
the French princess whose betrothal to Henry is offered as a peace-bribe — prefigures Act 5
worthless small fiefs — Henry's scornful view of the French dowry offer
does not please; is unwelcome
the quick-handed artilleryman
a forked staff holding the lighted match used to fire a cannon

glosses.phrase: eke out our performance with your mind
glosses.definition: supplement our staging with your imagination — the Chorus's signature plea

annotations.title: 'A city on th' inconstant billows dancing' — the fleet as sea-borne metropolis
annotations.body: The Act 3 Chorus is one of Shakespeare's great occasional poems in miniature. Its core image — the English fleet observed from shore, 'a city on th' inconstant billows dancing' — has been admired since the Romantics. The stanza does several things at once: it persuades the audience to visualize a vast invasion fleet the Globe cannot stage; it dignifies the expedition with civic grandeur ('so appears this fleet majestical'); and it uses shore-based spectatorship ('you stand upon the rivage') to make the audience an onlooker rather than a participant. Notice the verbs: 'grapple your minds to sternage of this navy,' 'Work, work your thoughts.' The Chorus demands work from us; the language is almost nautical labor. And the stanza's closing image — 'the nimble gunner / With linstock now the devilish cannon touches / And down goes all before them' — dispatches Harfleur's fall in a single graceful sentence, letting the imagination accomplish what the stage cannot.
sources: Samuel Taylor Coleridge, Lectures on Shakespeare (1818)

annotations.title: The Katherine offer — dowry as deflection
annotations.body: The Chorus introduces an offstage diplomatic detail: the French king has offered Katharine as Henry's bride, together with 'some petty and unprofitable dukedoms,' as a settlement. 'The offer likes not' — Henry refuses. Why this matters: it tells the audience that a marriage-solution to the war existed and was declined. When Act 5 finally stages the wooing scene, the audience should remember that Katherine was offered to Henry before Harfleur, before Agincourt, before the thousands of French dead. Henry accepted her only after taking France at sword-point. The Chorus's brief couplet — dowry proposal, Henry refuses, 'nimble gunner … devilish cannon touches' — is a compressed argument about what this war is actually buying.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)

trials.prompt: The Chorus compares the English fleet at sea to —
options: A city on the inconstant billows dancing — civic grandeur conferred on the invasion by imagery

trials.prompt: Why does the Chorus mention the French king's offer of Katherine as a peace-settlement?
options: To introduce Katherine as a character for the first time
To indicate that a marriage-settlement was offered BEFORE the war and was declined — reminding us (when we reach Act 5) that Katherine was the price of conquest, not the reward of genuine love
To stage a French marriage proposal
To introduce the sub-plot of Alice the interpreter

trials.prompt: How does the Chorus handle the fall of Harfleur?
options: Compresses it into a closing couplet — 'the nimble gunner / With linstock now the devilish cannon touches / And down goes all before them' — leaving the imagination to do the work
Narrates it through a captured French soldier
